mnemonics.title: Sibling Parent Edge
mnemonics.content: Remember RelativeLayout relationships: Align to SIBLING (layout_toLeftOf), PARENT (layout_alignParentTop), or EDGE (layout_alignStart)

mnemonics.title: Rows Eat Columns
mnemonics.content: TableLayout structure: ROWS contain columns (add TableRow first, then place views horizontally)

mnemonics.title: Vertical First, Then Columns
mnemonics.content: TableLayout hierarchy: Add VERTICAL TableRows first, then HORIZONTAL columns within each row

mnemonics.title: Weight vs Gravity
mnemonics.content: In TableLayout: Use layout_weight for COLUMN distribution, android:gravity for CELL content alignment

acronyms.usage: Remember key attributes: layout_alignRight, layout_alignEnd, layout_above, layout_toStartOf, etc.
RelativeLayout positioning directions relative to parent/siblings

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a family tree: Parent at center, children positioned relative to parent (above/below) or siblings (left/right)
Picture a chessboard - rows (TableRows) as horizontal lines, columns as vertical cells
Visualize rubber bands (stretchColumns) pulling table edges to fill screen width
See RelativeLayout views as boats anchored to specific points (parent edges or other views)

analogies.analogy: Like arranging furniture in a room - position sofa LEFT OF window, RIGHT OF coffee table, BELOW ceiling
Similar to spreadsheet cells - rows represent records, columns represent data fields
Like dividing pizza slices - layout_weight determines how much space each column gets relative to others
Think of train cars - each TableRow is a separate carriage holding horizontally aligned seats (views)
